stoic_theme: Epictetus taught that everything can be divided into what is 'up to us' — our judgments, intentions, and responses — and what is not, such as other people's opinions, our reputation, and outcomes beyond our effort. Peace comes from investing our energy only in the first category.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna tells Arjuna that he has a right to act, to do his work fully, but no claim on how that work turns out — the outcome was never his to own in the first place.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Kashinath Trimbak Telang's 1882 public-domain translation (Sacred Books of the East) and Edwin Arnold's 1885 'The Song Celestial'; verify exact phrasing against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where today did you mistake the uncontrollable for the controllable?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius kept mortality close at hand, not to induce dread but to sharpen attention — remembering that time is finite makes it harder to waste on pettiness and easier to act with the urgency and honesty a short life deserves.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna reminds Arjuna that the self was never born and does not die — the body changes and ends, but what is truly 'you' is not the perishable part, so facing death squarely need not mean despair.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882) and Annie Besant's 1905 translation; verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: If you kept today's mortality in view, what would you stop postponing?

stoic_theme: Epictetus advised against wishing that events unfold as we prefer, and instead training ourselves to wish that events unfold as they actually do — not passive resignation, but a redirection of desire toward reality itself.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna describes evenness of mind toward success and failure alike as the very definition of yoga — a steadiness that doesn't depend on things going our way.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where today did you resist 'what is' instead of meeting it evenly?

stoic_theme: Seneca recommended rehearsing setbacks and losses in the mind before they happen — not to invite misfortune, but so that when difficulty arrives it finds us already acquainted with the shape of it, and less able to catch us by surprise.
verse_paraphrase: In the Devi Gita section of the text, the Goddess describes how all created forms eventually dissolve back into their source — a teaching meant to loosen our grip on permanence, not to frighten.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 public-domain translation of the Srimad Devi Bhagavatam; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: What are you assuming will last that might not?

stoic_theme: Epictetus separated the raw event from our judgment about it, arguing that what disturbs us is rarely the event itself but the verdict we silently attach to it — and that verdict is the one thing always within our power to examine and revise.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna tells Arjuna that sensations like heat and cold, pleasure and pain, are temporary contacts that come and go — and that learning to bear them without being ruled by them is part of maturing.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What judgment did you add to today's events that wasn't actually in the events themselves?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius practiced imagining himself from a great height — watching human affairs, his own included, shrink to their true proportion — as a way of loosening the grip of whatever felt urgent up close.
verse_paraphrase: The Goddess distinguishes the true Self from the body and mind it temporarily inhabits, pointing to an identity far wider than any single situation or role.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: Zoomed out, how much space does today's worry actually take up?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius observed that what blocks an action can become part of the action — an obstacle in the path often becomes the path, once we stop treating it as pure loss and start treating it as raw material.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna says every undertaking carries some flaw, the way fire comes wrapped in smoke — and that this is a reason to keep doing the work well, not a reason to abandon it.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What flawed-but-worth-doing task have you been avoiding because it isn't clean?

stoic_theme: The early Stoics held that character — wisdom, courage, justice, self-control — is the only thing genuinely good, and that everything else commonly prized (wealth, health, reputation) is 'preferred' but not what makes a life good or bad.
stoic_source_ref: Stoic doctrine as transmitted through Epictetus's teaching
verse_paraphrase: The Goddess links right conduct and inner cultivation directly to devotion, treating an ethical life itself as a form of worship rather than a separate obligation.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: Where today did you treat something external as more important than your own conduct?

stoic_theme: Seneca practiced deliberately living for short stretches as if poor — plain food, coarse clothes — so that hardship, if it ever came uninvited, would already be familiar rather than frightening.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna describes yoga as suited to one who is moderate in eating, recreation, and sleep — sorrow, he says, is destroyed for the one who lives with measure rather than excess.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where could you choose 'less' on purpose today, before life chooses it for you?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius described a 'ruling faculty' at the center of the self — the part that judges, chooses, and assents — that no external force can enter uninvited; it can be neglected, but it cannot be conquered from outside.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna tells Arjuna to lift himself by his own effort, not to let himself sink, since the self can be either one's best friend or one's own worst enemy.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Today, was your own mind more your ally or your adversary?

stoic_theme: The Stoics described themselves as citizens of the world before citizens of any city or nation, holding that shared reason binds all people together more fundamentally than the borders that happen to separate them.
stoic_source_ref: Marcus Aurelius, Meditations, Book 4; Epictetus, Discourses
verse_paraphrase: The Goddess teaches that the one Self underlies every being without exception, so the sense of a truly separate 'other' is, at the deepest level, a case of mistaken identity.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: Who did you treat as fundamentally 'other' today, and what would change if you didn't?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius saw the cosmos as a single organism whose parts — including other people — are built to work together, so acting against another person is, in a real sense, acting against a part of oneself.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna describes the practiced yogi as one who sees the Self present equally in all beings, and all beings equally within the Self, no longer drawing a hard line between 'self' and 'other'.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where today could you have acted with the other person's interest as your own?

stoic_theme: Epictetus taught that wanting fewer things, and wanting only what is truly within reach, is what makes a person free — desire aimed at what's beyond our control guarantees frustration no matter how things turn out.
verse_paraphrase: The Goddess describes yoga as steadiness of mind through the pull of opposites — pleasure and pain, gain and loss — achieved by no longer craving one side of each pair over the other.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: What are you craving right now that, if released, would loosen its grip on you?

stoic_concept: Askesis: Training the Mind Like an Athlete
stoic_theme: Epictetus treated philosophy as a discipline to be trained the way an athlete trains a body — through repeated practice, not through insight alone, since knowing the right thing to do is not the same as being able to do it under pressure.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna agrees with Arjuna that the mind is genuinely hard to control and restless, but insists it can still be mastered — through steady practice and by loosening its grip on outcomes.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What's one small, repeatable practice — not a single act of willpower — that would actually change how you respond?

stoic_concept: Only the Present Moment Can Be Lost
stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius pointed out that no one loses any life but the one they are living right now — the past is already gone and the future isn't yet ours, so worrying about losing 'more' time than this moment is a kind of category error.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna compares the self to someone who sets aside worn-out clothes for new ones — the body changes and ends, but this is described as a natural passing-on rather than a true loss.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882) and Arnold (1885); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What are you treating as a bigger loss than it actually is, measured against only what's real: this moment?

stoic_concept: Understanding Others' Errors as Ignorance, Not Malice
stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius reminded himself each morning that he would meet people who act badly out of ignorance of what is truly good, not out of genuine malice — which made it easier to respond without matching their agitation.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna explains that all action is really carried out by the underlying qualities of nature, while a person confused by ego mistakenly believes 'I am the one doing this' — a kind of ignorance about how action actually arises.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where today did you assume malice where ignorance or conditioning was the more likely explanation?

stoic_theme: The early Stoics, following Zeno, held that a good life means living in agreement with nature — understanding how things truly work, including our own nature as reasoning, social beings, and acting accordingly rather than against the grain of reality.
stoic_source_ref: Zeno of Citium, as transmitted by Diogenes Laertius
verse_paraphrase: Krishna observes that even a wise person acts according to their own nature, since all beings follow their nature — and asks what good forcing restraint against it would really do.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where are you fighting your own nature today instead of working with it?

stoic_theme: The Stoics taught that things like health, wealth, and reputation are 'indifferent' to a good life in the strict sense — worth preferring when available, but not what actually makes a life good or bad, which depends entirely on character.
stoic_source_ref: Stoic doctrine, as taught by Epictetus
verse_paraphrase: Krishna tells Arjuna to hold pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat as equal, and then engage fully in the task at hand — acting this way, he says, incurs no wrongdoing.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Which outcome today have you been treating as 'good or bad' that's really just preferred or not?

stoic_theme: Epictetus compared life to a play where we don't choose our role — short or long, rich or poor — but we are fully responsible for how well we play the part we've actually been given.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna teaches that it is better to do one's own duty imperfectly than to do another's duty well, since acting in line with one's own nature does not bring the same fault.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Are you trying to play someone else's role today instead of your own, even a well-intentioned one?

stoic_concept: Freedom From Fear of Others' Opinions
stoic_theme: Epictetus taught that other people's judgments of us are not up to us and therefore not worth the anxiety we give them — a free person is measured by their own standards, not by staying popular.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna describes the person dear to him as one who is the same in honor and dishonor, unmoved equally by praise and blame.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Whose opinion are you giving more authority over your choices today than it deserves?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius pictured time as a river sweeping everything along, with each new thing barely arriving before it is carried off and replaced — a reminder that clinging to any fixed state is already out of step with how things work.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna describes beings as unseen before birth, seen for a while in between, and unseen again after death — asking why this ordinary pattern should be a cause for grief.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What are you gripping tightly today that was always going to be temporary?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius reasoned that whatever harms the community ultimately harms its members too, since a person is part of a larger whole the way a limb is part of a body — so acting for the common good is really acting in one's own genuine interest.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna points to figures like Janaka who reached fulfillment through action alone, and urges Arjuna to act with an eye toward holding the world together.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What's one action today that would serve the wider group, not just your own immediate interest?

stoic_theme: The Stoics practiced treating whatever came their way — welcome or not — as enough, cultivating contentment as a discipline rather than waiting for circumstances to arrange themselves favorably first.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna describes one who is content with whatever comes without seeking it, who has moved beyond the pull of opposites and is free of envy, remaining steady in both success and failure.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What do you already have today that would count as 'enough' if you let it?

stoic_theme: Epictetus located true freedom in what he called prohairesis — the faculty of choice itself — arguing that this alone can never be taken from a person by force, even when everything external is stripped away.
verse_paraphrase: Having laid out his teaching in full, Krishna tells Arjuna to reflect on it completely and then act as he himself chooses — leaving the final decision to Arjuna's own will.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where today is the real choice still entirely yours, no matter what else is happening around you?

stoic_concept: Enduring Pain and Hardship with Equanimity
stoic_theme: Epictetus treated illness and physical hardship as tests that reveal whether one's philosophy is real — the body can be constrained, he argued, without the mind's own composure needing to be disturbed along with it.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna defines yoga in part as the state of being disconnected from union with suffering — a severance from pain, known by this name.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where is discomfort today asking to be endured steadily, rather than escaped immediately?

stoic_theme: The Stoics treated attraction and aversion to sensory pleasure as natural forces that, left unchecked, quietly run a person's life — so self-discipline meant noticing that pull rather than being run by it.
verse_paraphrase: Krishna warns that attraction and aversion toward sense objects sit in the senses themselves, and one should not fall under their control, since they act as obstacles on the path.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where today did a small pull toward or away from something quietly make a decision for you?

stoic_concept: Courage in the Face of Adversity
stoic_theme: The Stoics counted courage among the core virtues — not the absence of fear, but the refusal to let fear or weakness of heart be the deciding factor in what one does.
stoic_source_ref: Stoic doctrine, on courage as a cardinal virtue
verse_paraphrase: Krishna tells Arjuna not to give in to unmanly weakness, urging him to cast off this faintheartedness and rise to meet what is in front of him.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: What is fear quietly talking you out of today that courage would still do?

stoic_theme: The Stoic practice of prosoche meant keeping careful watch over one's own attention moment to moment, the way a guard stays alert at a post — since a wandering, unwatched mind is where trouble quietly gets in.
stoic_source_ref: Epictetus and later Stoic practice, prosoche (attention)
verse_paraphrase: Krishna instructs that whenever the restless, unsteady mind wanders off, it should be gently brought back and settled under one's own watchful control.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Telang (1882); verify against a preferred edition.
bridge_prompt: Where has your attention wandered off today without your noticing until just now?

stoic_theme: Seneca argued that life is not actually short — we simply waste much of it on distraction and postponement, so that by the time we go looking for the time to truly live, most of it is already gone.
stoic_source_ref: Seneca, On the Shortness of Life
verse_paraphrase: The Devi Gita section describes Maya, the Goddess's power of illusion, as what makes the fleeting seem lasting — the same confusion that lets time slip by unnoticed as though it were unlimited.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: What have you been postponing as though you had unlimited time for it?

stoic_theme: Epictetus separated desire and aversion from action, arguing that most unhappiness comes from desiring things outside our control — the discipline is in redirecting desire itself toward only what is genuinely up to us.
verse_paraphrase: The Devi Gita presents right desire and non-attachment together as part of the same discipline — wanting rightly and holding loosely are taught as two sides of one practice.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: What desire today would be worth redirecting rather than simply indulging or suppressing?

stoic_concept: The Sage's Equanimity Amid Praise and Blame
stoic_theme: The Stoic ideal of the sage was someone whose composure did not rise and fall with others' approval — praised or criticized, the sage's inner state stayed essentially the same.
stoic_source_ref: Stoic doctrine, the ideal of the sage (apatheia)
verse_paraphrase: The Devi Gita describes the steady devotee as one whose mind holds its ground regardless of outer circumstance, neither lifted by favor nor shaken by its absence.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: Whose praise or criticism moved you today more than it should have?

stoic_theme: Seneca and Epictetus both practiced deliberately needing less — not as deprivation, but because wanting less leaves less for fear and circumstance to threaten.
stoic_source_ref: Seneca and Epictetus, on simple living
verse_paraphrase: The Devi Gita's guidance on disciplined, modest living treats simplicity of habit as a direct support for inner steadiness, not as a hardship to endure.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: What could you need less of today, on purpose, to have less for circumstance to threaten?

stoic_theme: Marcus Aurelius found steadiness in remembering he was one small part of a vast, ordered whole — not the center of it — and that accepting this place honestly was itself a source of peace rather than diminishment.
verse_paraphrase: The Devi Gita's concluding teaching frames surrender to the larger cosmic order not as a loss of self, but as the resolution the entire teaching has been building toward.
translation_source_note: Paraphrase informed by Swami Vijnanananda's 1921-23 translation; chapter-level citation only — verify exact shloka before treating as precise.
bridge_prompt: Where today would accepting your actual place in things — no bigger, no smaller — bring relief?
